text: Hi, this is a patient that had a Roux-en-Y bypass approximately a month ago at a nearby hospital. She presented to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain. She had an x-ray, showed free air under the diaphragm, CAT scan that showed Perforation possible at the JJ anastomosis with no evidence of obstruction. The conduit looked normal size. Everything looked great apart from a lot of air next to the JJ anastomosis.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to perform a diagnostic laparoscopy, removing the greater omentum to visualize the JJ anastomosis. The perforation at the JJ anastomosis is visible, with the Roux limb appearing short, approximately 100 centimeters, and the BP limb measuring around 50 to 60 centimeters.
The surgeon runs the biliopancreatic limb (BPLM) and identifies the ligament of Treitz, examining the area for signs of perforation.

The surgeon evaluates the common channel laparoscopically, confirming no evidence of obstruction. The ICG imaging appears normal. The surgeon uses scissors and a VESA sealer to thin the tissue at the GJ anastomosis, performing a leak test which shows no abnormalities. Air is inflated through the NG tube, and a scope is prepared but not used.

The surgeon repairs the anastomosis, which was created in an anti-colic, anti-gastric fashion. Indocyanine green (ICG) imaging confirms good blood flow. The repair is reinforced with an omental patch placed on top, and the mucosa appears healthy.

The surgeon attempts to elevate the gastric remnant but encounters tension, leading to the release of the omentum attachment to the stomach. A 20 French balloon G-tube is inserted, and the stomach is positioned against the posterior aspect of the anterior abdominal wall. The procedure includes a thorough laparoscopic washout and the placement of multiple drains in the right upper quadrant, left upper quadrant, and pelvis.
